title: Physical Quantities & SI Units
subtitle: The 7 base units that measure everything in the universe

blocks.heading: Why do we need standard units?
blocks.body: To measure something is to compare it with an agreed standard called a unit. Saying 'the rope is 5' is meaningless; '5 metres' is not. The world agrees on one system — the SI (Système International). It has just seven base units, and every other unit (for speed, force, energy…) is a derived unit built by combining them.

blocks.heading: The 7 SI Base Units
blocks.body: Length → metre (m) | Mass → kilogram (kg) | Time → second (s) | Electric current → ampere (A) | Temperature → kelvin (K) | Amount of substance → mole (mol) | Luminous intensity → candela (cd)

blocks.heading: GPS and the need for precision
blocks.body: GPS satellites use SI units for timing signals accurate to nanoseconds. A 1 microsecond error in timing equals a 300-metre position error on the ground. Without the globally agreed SI second, navigation systems would give wildly different results.

blocks.heading: Convert your height
blocks.body: Measure your height in cm, then convert to metres, then to km. Notice how the number changes but you don't. That is the whole point of units — they carry the information about the scale of measurement.

blocks.heading: What if every country used different base units?
blocks.body: In 1999, NASA lost the Mars Climate Orbiter because one team sent data in pound-force·seconds and another expected newton·seconds. The spacecraft missed Mars orbit and was destroyed. Without shared units, international science and engineering would be impossible — and dangerous.

quiz.question: Which SI unit measures electric current?
quiz.explanation: Ampere (A) is the SI base unit for electric current. Volt, Watt and Ohm are all derived units.

quiz.question: How many base SI units are there?
quiz.explanation: There are exactly 7 SI base units: metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole and candela.

quiz.question: The SI unit of temperature is:
quiz.explanation: Kelvin (K) is the SI base unit of thermodynamic temperature. Celsius and Fahrenheit are common but not SI base units.
